item.title: Why it’s time to change Japan’s constitution
item.description: Amending the text would be a sign of the country’s flexibility in an era of uncertainty

item.title: America owes its European allies a defence roadmap
item.description: Retaliatory cutbacks are no way to achieve proper burden-shifting in Nato

item.title: The French guide to not working in May
item.description: ‘Faire le pont’ — the art of maximising public holidays — is about to reach its zenith

item.title: Novo Nordisk gets a second chance at weight-loss supremacy
item.description: The Danish drugmaker appears to have the edge in GLP-1 pills

item.title: The second world war fixation gets the US into trouble again
item.description: The Iran war is the latest mess to spring from the idea that ‘appeasement’ is always wrong

item.title: Row over Green candidates’ comments lays bare party’s real danger
item.description: Criticism of social media posts will not decide elections — but the quality of vetting matters for competency

item.title: Gilts and UK local elections: Bad vibes, bad timing
item.description: Plus, more on emerging markets

item.title: Vodafone’s slow-burn strategy is starting to pay off
item.description: The share price has picked up and its valuation as a multiple of forecast earnings has risen to the top of European telcos

item.title: Conned by a chatbot
item.description: Like tricksters, LLMs have perfected the art of plausibility

item.title: Imbalances are back on the global agenda
item.description: Policymakers must overcome the mercantilist fallacy that the way to get rich is by running surpluses forever

item.title: Don’t let the taxman grab your tax-free cash
item.description: It’s tempting to blow the lump sum on a sports car — but the more sensible option may be to take it in small amounts like regular income

item.title: US shale oil producers are wise to avoid the temptation of higher prices
item.description: With little slack in the system, soaring prices don’t give operators that much of an incentive to let rip

item.title: We must be mindful of the risks of private credit
item.description: The sector remains untested in a severe or prolonged economic downturn

item.title: Why the UAE really left Opec
item.description: Leaving the oil cartel is not just a commercial decision — it reflects structural changes in global energy markets

item.title: Uzbekistan’s London IPO brings frontier markets closer to home
item.description: Resource-rich nation’s move into foreign equity markets could tempt investors with the stomach for risk

item.title: Public and private markets vie for gains from AI job disruption
item.description: Corporate leaders bet automation will produce outsized returns

item.title: When rare viruses return
item.description: A suspected outbreak of hantavirus is a reminder of the perils of health emergencies in enclosed spaces

item.title: Bypassing the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Investing in alternatives to this and other maritime chokepoints is costly but necessary

item.title: Why the Iran war might not spur a faster transition to low-carbon energy
item.description: Increased priority on security of power supply might see some countries continue to rely on fossil fuels

item.title: Central banks prepare for a long war
item.description: Officials are softening people up for difficult decisions ahead. Also in this newsletter, how oil prices work

item.title: The age of the American Pharaoh
item.description: It would be strange if dynastic succession were not on Trump’s mind

item.title: US bankruptcy fights are heading to London
item.description: The use of foreign venues is the new frontier for debt restructurings at American companies

item.title: Great expectations (for earnings)
item.description: Stocks look cheaper — if everything goes perfectly

item.title: How not to defeat antisemitism
item.description: The only way to overcome this scourge is to see it for what it is: racism

item.title: Welcome to the Great Hunkering Down
item.description: Why aren’t more people quitting their jobs?
